continuity-of-self->sleep-wake-conflict: Weak continuity of self can make morning startup feel like a full reboot. Night then becomes the only place continuity feels recoverable, so delaying sleep to protect that thread becomes more likely.
sleep-wake-conflict->continuity-of-self: Sleep debt raises morning startup friction and fragments memory carryover, which can make continuity of self feel even less stable across days.
continuity-of-self->decision-paralysis: When continuity of self is unstable, future-you can feel like a stranger. That weakens commitment to longer-horizon choices and increases decision stall, especially under uncertainty.
interoception->decision-paralysis: If body signals are muted or ambiguous, it is harder to sense what future-you will need (rest, food, pace, recovery). Choices lose emotional clarity and become cognitively over-analysed.
reward-volatility->decision-paralysis: When reward salience keeps shifting, long-horizon priorities lose traction. The brain keeps reopening the choice set, making commitment and sequencing much harder.
sensory-prediction-load->sleep-wake-conf: High sensory uncertainty keeps threat-monitoring active into the evening. Bedtime then feels less like shutdown and more like vigilance, delaying sleep onset and reinforcing late-night wakefulness.
proprioceptive-calibration->sleep-wake-c: If body-in-space signals stay noisy, the nervous system keeps seeking movement, pressure, or repositioning to feel settled. That calibration search can delay sleep onset and fragment shutdown.
proprioceptive-calibration->decision-par: When posture, tension, and movement cues are hard to read, future-body needs are harder to forecast. Decisions about timing, pacing, and effort stay uncertain and easier to postpone.
proprioceptive-calibration->musculoskele: When body-map signals are inconsistent, compensation patterns become the default. Over time, repeated compensation can shift load into vulnerable joints and tissues.
morning-startup-lag->sleep-wake-conflict: When mornings require a long activation ramp, nights feel like the only period of fully coherent function. That perceived payoff can reinforce delayed sleep timing.
sleep-wake-conflict->morning-startup-lag: Short or delayed sleep increases sleep inertia and lowers early executive access, stretching the next morning's startup ramp.
morning-startup-lag->decision-paralysis: If early-state calibration is incomplete, priorities are harder to sequence and commit to. Small decisions stall until activation catches up.
emotional-signal-decoding-latency->alexi: Delayed emotional decoding is an upstream mechanism that commonly presents as alexithymia in lived experience: feelings are present, but labeling lags behind sensation.
alexithymia->emotional-signal-decoding-l: Alexithymia can be read as the lived expression of slower emotional-signal decoding, especially under overload or fast social demands.
working-memory-reliability->continuity-o: When active self-state context drops from working memory, continuity of self weakens and identity can feel discontinuous across time.
working-memory-reliability->emotional-pe: When active relational context drops from working memory, emotional continuity weakens and people can feel less psychologically present between interactions.
emotional-permanence->working-memory-rel: Instability in emotional permanence often reflects upstream working-memory reliability limits in maintaining active social and self-referential threads.
continuity-of-self->working-memory-relia: Fragmented continuity of self often reflects upstream working-memory reliability limits in maintaining cross-time self-representation.
musculoskeletal-strain->proprioceptive-c: Persistent musculoskeletal strain often signals long-term compensation for unresolved proprioceptive uncertainty, especially under fatigue and repeated transitions.
